title: Cross-Browser Activity Correlation
description: Evidence spans Chrome and Firefox profiles on the same machine. Build a unified timeline to correlate visits, searches, and tab activity across browsers.
investigationObjective: Build expertise in cross-browser correlation techniques by integrating artefacts from multiple browser profiles to reconstruct a unified investigative timeline.

events.description: [Firefox] Visited https://www.amazon.co.uk/gift-finder (title: Amazon.co.uk – Gift Finder, visit count: 1, first visit: 2024-09-14)
events.explanation: Unrelated online shopping activity — a single-visit Amazon page with no repeat engagement, consistent with casual browsing.

events.description: [Chrome] Visited https://jira.techflow.io/browse/DEPLOY-482 (title: DEPLOY-482 – Production Release | TechFlow Jira, visit count: 3, last visit: 2024-09-13, assignee: alex.thompson)
events.explanation: Chrome activity began with a Jira ticket for a production release, anchoring the investigation timeline. The ticket assignee (alex.thompson) ties the activity to a specific user account.

relationships.label: The Jira ticket often motivates related research or preparation in another browser.
Production release review can precede downloading deployment artefacts.

hints: Think about which event usually anchors a deployment-related investigation.
A Jira ticket for a production release may drive related research activity elsewhere.
The Chrome Jira ticket visit should be placed first, before any Firefox research activity begins.

events.description: [Firefox] Google search: "best coffee shops Edinburgh city centre"
events.explanation: Unrelated personal search for coffee shops — a common midday break search pattern unrelated to any technical investigation.

events.description: [Firefox] Google search: "kubernetes rolling update zero downtime deployment" (engine: Google, locale: en-GB)
events.explanation: Firefox shows a technical search consistent with preparing for the deployment referenced in Chrome. The en-GB locale confirms the same user context across browsers.

relationships.label: A technical search commonly leads to the referenced documentation page.

hints: Think about what happens when someone wants to learn about a concept related to their ticket.
A technical search often precedes opening the supporting documentation.
The Firefox Kubernetes search should be placed right after the Jira ticket visit and before the docs page is opened.

events.description: [Firefox] Visited https://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/kubernetes-basics/update/update-intro/ (title: Kubernetes: Performing a Rolling Update, load time: 1.2s, cached: false)
events.explanation: The Firefox search led to official Kubernetes documentation on rolling updates. The uncached load time and slow connection (1.2s for a doc page) may indicate the user spent time reading rather than just loading.

relationships.label: Documentation research in one browser often continues alongside authentication in another.

hints: Think about which page usually appears immediately after a documentation search.
Technical documentation pages often follow focused searches.
The Kubernetes docs page should be placed right after the Firefox search and before the Chrome Jira session cookie is set.

events.description: [Firefox] Google search: "twitter trending topics today uk"
events.explanation: Unrelated social media browsing — checking trending topics during a break, a common non-work activity pattern.

events.description: [Chrome] Cookie set: JSESSIONID on jira.techflow.io (Name: JSESSIONID, Value: 9A3F2B1C4D5E6F7A8B9C0D1E2F3A4B5C, Domain: jira.techflow.io, HttpOnly, SameSite=Relaxed, Path=/, expires: session)
events.explanation: A fresh Jira session cookie in Chrome indicates the browser re-authenticated to Jira during the deployment review. The JSESSIONID value format matches the Atlassian standard session token pattern.

relationships.label: Session cookies usually appear after accessing the target application.
An authenticated session often precedes downloading protected artefacts.

hints: Think about which cookie activity reflects returning to a protected application.
Session cookies often indicate an authenticated browser session during deployment review.
The Chrome JSESSIONID cookie should be placed right after the Jira ticket visit and before the deploy config download.

events.description: [Firefox] Visited https://www.netflix.com/browse (title: Netflix – Browse, profile: alex_t, autoplay: on)
events.explanation: Unrelated entertainment streaming activity — the Netflix profile name (alex_t) matches the Jira assignee, but Netflix browsing is clearly personal and unrelated to the deployment investigation.

events.description: [Chrome] Downloaded deploy-config-prod-v2.4.yaml to C:\Users\adevains\Downloads\ (size: 4.2 KB, type: text/plain, browser: Chrome 130.0.6099.130, etag: "8c3f1a2b-4d5e-6f7a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c", accessed: 2024-09-14T10:23:20Z)
events.explanation: A production deployment configuration file was downloaded via Chrome, creating a critical artefact. The file was accessed almost immediately after download (accessed: 2024-09-14T10:23:20Z), indicating the user opened or used it right away.

relationships.label: Downloading protected artefacts often follows an authenticated session.
Downloading a deployment configuration often precedes further deployment checklist research.

hints: Think about which download typically follows after authentication on Jira.
Downloading production configuration is a strong indicator of deployment preparation.
The Chrome deploy config download should be placed right after the JSESSIONID cookie and before the wiki checklist search.

events.description: [Firefox] Google search: "stackoverflow error handling react useeffect cleanup"
events.explanation: Unrelated programming Q&A research — a common developer behaviour unrelated to the deployment investigation.

events.description: [Chrome] Google search: "techflow internal wiki deployment checklist SOP" (engine: Google, locale: en-GB, signed-in: alex.thompson@techflow.io)
events.explanation: Chrome was used to locate the internal deployment checklist on the company wiki. The signed-in Google account (alex.thompson@techflow.io) matches the Jira assignee, confirming the same user across Chrome, Firefox, and the Google account.

relationships.label: A deployment checklist search typically leads to the checklist page.

hints: Think about what a user searches for next when preparing for a deployment.
A deployment checklist search often precedes opening the internal wiki page.
The Chrome checklist search should be placed right after the deploy config download and right before the wiki checklist page is opened.

events.description: [Chrome] Visited https://wiki.techflow.io/display/OPS/Production+Deployment+Checklist (title: OPS – Production Deployment Checklist, author: ops-team, last modified: 2024-09-10, version: 3.2, access level: Internal)
events.explanation: The internal wiki checklist page was accessed immediately after the Chrome search. The page metadata (author, version 3.2, last modified 2024-09-10) provides forensic context about the document's currency and ownership.

relationships.label: Checklist access in Chrome can correlate with wiki authentication in Firefox.

hints: Think about which page comes right after a deployment checklist search.
A checklist page visit often represents a direct follow-up to a checklist search.
The Chrome wiki checklist visit should be placed right after the search and before the Firefox wiki session cookie is created.

events.description: [Firefox] Visited https://www.linkedin.com/feed (title: LinkedIn – Feed, profile: Alex Thompson, connection count: 342)
events.explanation: Unrelated professional networking activity — checking LinkedIn during a development session, a common non-work browsing pattern.

events.description: [Firefox] Cookie set: wiki_session on wiki.techflow.io (Name: wiki_session, Value: fx_a1b2c3d4e5f6, Domain: wiki.techflow.io, HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict, Path=/, expires: 2024-09-21T10:28:54Z)
events.explanation: Firefox independently authenticated to the wiki using a different session cookie than Chrome, demonstrating parallel cross-browser access to internal resources. The session expiry in 7 days and Strict SameSite indicate a deliberate persistent login.

relationships.label: Wiki authentication in Firefox can align with Chrome wiki access.
Wiki session cookies often precede further navigation to protected resources.

hints: Think about which cookie activity reflects an authenticated browser session on the wiki.
Wiki session cookies often follow authentication or page access activity.
The Firefox wiki_session cookie should be placed right after the Chrome wiki visit and before the Firefox Jenkins job page.

events.description: [Firefox] Visited https://ci.techflow.io/job/production-deploy/ (title: Jenkins – production-deploy #117, build status: SUCCESS, duration: 4m 23s, triggered by: alex.thompson)
events.explanation: Firefox navigated to the Jenkins CI job page, indicating the deployment pipeline was monitored from a second browser. The build status (SUCCESS) and the trigger user (alex.thompson) confirm this is an active, completed deployment under the same user account.

relationships.label: CI monitoring often follows authentication to supporting resources.
CI job monitoring often precedes saving or downloading related build artefacts.

hints: Think about which page a developer opens to monitor an in-progress deployment.
Monitoring a CI job often reflects continued deployment investigation.
The Firefox Jenkins job page should be placed right after the wiki_session cookie and right before the console log download.

events.description: [Firefox] Downloaded jenkins-build-117-console.log to C:\Users\adevains\Downloads\ (size: 128 KB, type: text/plain, browser: Firefox 130.0, MIME: text/plain, last modified: 2024-09-14T10:33:48Z)
events.explanation: The Jenkins console log was saved in Firefox, completing the cross-browser evidence chain for the deployment event. The log file was last modified at the exact same timestamp as the download, confirming a freshly generated build artefact.

relationships.label: Saving a console log often follows monitoring the related CI job page.

hints: Think about which artefact a developer might save after watching a CI job run.
Saving a CI console log often follows monitoring the related job page.
The Firefox Jenkins console log download should be placed last, immediately after the CI job page visit.

events.description: [Firefox] Google search: "how to tie a half-Windsor knot"
events.explanation: Unrelated personal grooming query — a random non-technical search during or after the deployment session, unrelated to any investigation activity.

events.description: [Chrome] Visited https://jira.techflow.io/browse/DEPLOY-482 (title: DEPLOY-482 – Production Release, status: RESOLVED, resolution: Released, build: #117)
events.explanation: Chrome returned to the Jira ticket after the deployment completed, confirming the release was finalised and the ticket status updated to RESOLVED with build #117 — matching the Jenkins job number.

relationships.label: Returning to a Jira ticket after CI monitoring confirms the deployment loop is complete.

hints: Think about what a developer does after downloading a build log and completing the deployment.
Finalising the Jira ticket after the build log download confirms the deployment is complete.
The final Jira ticket visit should be placed last, immediately after the Jenkins console log download.
